parts.title: Part 1
parts.annotated_transcript_html:
Peter: It depends on the room you have. I have the large bedroom, so I pay $110 a week. Yours is $80 because it's a bit smaller. [Q1]

Jim: I've got a car. By the way, is there anywhere I can park?

Peter: Well, you're lucky because this house has a garage. [Q2] At the moment there are some empty boxes in there, but there's still plenty of room for your car.

Jim: Now, what about phone calls? I didn't see a phone in the hall when I came in.

Peter: Yeah, it's in the kitchen. [Q3] It was in the living room, but it was hard to hear over the television.

Peter: It's a nice room and there's a cupboard and desk in there already. But it does get quite cold at night in that room and it doesn't have a heater. [Q4]

Jim: No problem. My friend has just given me one, so I'll bring that. [Q4]

Peter: Well, we've just got a microwave and my mother bought me a rice cooker, but it would be great if you had a toaster. [Q5] Mine's just broken.

Peter: I'm a keen sports fan and I usually watch the game on TV on Friday evening. [Q6] Would that be OK?

Jim: Me too, so that's good. [Q6] And I work at the supermarket on Tuesday and Thursday nights. [Q7]

Peter: Mine are in the afternoons too. You could give me a lift. We can share the petrol. [Q8]

Peter: Well, if you're interested and the rent is OK, I need someone by the 1st of June [Q9] when the new rent period starts.

Jim: I've got an exam next week, so I'll move in after that if that's OK. [Q10]

parts.title: Part 2
parts.annotated_transcript_html:
Anne Rawson: Children are expected to arrive at school between 8.45 and 8.50 and the bell is rung for everybody to go to their classrooms at 8.55 so that we can take the register. [Q11]

Anne Rawson: Lunch is served in the canteen and children can bring their own packed lunch or a hot meal is available. [Q12]

Anne Rawson: In the middle of the morning and the middle of the afternoon, there are two breaks of 15 minutes [Q13] when the children also generally go outside, unless it's wet.

Anne Rawson: For example, on Thursday afternoons, rather than continuing with lessons, we expect the whole school to do sports. [Q14]

Anne Rawson: So, for each of the three terms, they are expected to produce a poster. [Q15]

Anne Rawson: We also make sure we keep a close eye on how each child is doing in their schoolwork and every month, rather than the teacher giving the children a report, as I know some schools do, they have to do tests. [Q16]

Anne Rawson: At the end of the school day, many of them have set up clubs that the children go to for an extra hour to learn a new skill, such as music. [Q17]

Anne Rawson: We're here at the main entrance and on the right, where you would normally expect to find reception, you'll see the Head's office. [Q18]

Anne Rawson: On the left of the classrooms, we have converted one of the old cloakrooms to create a music room. [Q19]

Anne Rawson: We used to be squashed into the old assembly hall next door, which, as you can see, has now been turned into a gym for the children. [Q20]

parts.title: Part 3
parts.annotated_transcript_html:
Jen: Then we can add something about what Forensic Linguists actually do to back it up. You know, like analysing documents to find out who wrote them. [Q21]

Rob: Where we're really going to get in trouble is that the recording is very unclear. [Q22]

Rob: They might do something like speaking with another accent because the tutor suggests they do that just to test us. [Q23]

Rob: Oh, and don't start your usual self-doubt about whether you can do the work or not. [Q24]

Jen: My problem is creating things to worry about before I've even started. [Q24]

Rob: Research Methods in Forensic Linguistics. I guess we ought to borrow that. [Q25/26]

Jen: But we should get out Modern Forensic Techniques. [Q25/26]

Rob: I'm not sure about how we're going to record the voices. [Q27/28] I'm not too good on the technical side of things.

Jen: Me neither. [Q27/28]

Rob: I'm more worried about fitting in the writing up. [Q27/28]

Jen: I know. We've got loads of other assignments to finish as well. [Q27/28]

Jen: Are you any good at introductions? They always seem to let me down.

Rob: OK. Leave that to me. [Q29/30]

Jen: I don't mind writing the methodology section if you feel comfortable doing the discussion and conclusions. [Q29/30]

parts.title: Part 4
parts.annotated_transcript_html:
Lecturer: Dormancy is a state of inactivity that organisms or living things enter when they need to conserve the resources available to them [Q31] and reduce their demands on their environment.

Lecturer: Animals that have the ability to become dormant can survive conditions which are stressful in environmental terms. [Q32]

Lecturer: The amount of competition they have to deal with from other creatures is greatly reduced. [Q33]

Lecturer: The ones that are the most significant are changes in temperature and the amount of light [Q34] organisms get.

Lecturer: Insects can experience a reduction in their metabolism [Q35], which is a form of dormancy called diapause.

Lecturer: When the water supply is low, the African lungfish burrows deep into the mud. [Q36]

Lecturer: While dormant, it faces two dangers. One is the possibility of starvation. [Q37]

Lecturer: Snails that remain outside during their dormancy can usually be recognised by a rather brightly coloured shell reflecting the sunlight and so minimising evaporation. [Q38]

Lecturer: This squirrel digs underground and builds a nest that is insulated. [Q39]

Lecturer: The area around the heart warms more quickly than the rest of the animal. [Q40]
